preFix.title: Insulating the floor above an integral garage
preFix.subtitle: Rigid board or mineral wool between joists to stop heat leaving through the garage ceiling
preFix.excerpt: The floor between a living room and an unheated integral garage is often uninsulated. Heat loss through this junction is equivalent to an uninsulated external wall. This is the sequence for fitting rigid PIR board or mineral wool batts between the floor joists from below, with a vapour control layer and fire-resistant lining where required.
preFix.sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Part L1B (2022 edition) on floor insulation targets. Approved Document B (fire safety) on fire-resistance of separating floors between integral garages and habitable rooms. Energy Saving Trust suspended floor insulation guidance.

content.text: An integral garage with a living room above is one of the most common cold-floor situations in UK homes built between 1960 and 2000. The floor joists span from the house wall to the garage wall, and the living room floor is the only separation between a heated room and an unheated space that may reach 0°C in winter. Adding 100 mm of PIR or 150 mm of mineral wool batts within the joist bays brings the floor in line with current retrofit targets and eliminates the cold-floor effect in the room above.
Fire resistance: the non-negotiable requirement
The separation between an integral garage and a habitable room must have 30 minutes'
fire resistance

under Approved Document B. If the existing ceiling below the living room floor is 12.5 mm plasterboard, it meets this requirement. If it is bare chipboard or no board at all, you must add a fire-resistant lining before or as part of the insulation work. Use 12.5 mm plasterboard with taped and jointed seams. Do not substitute OSB or ply for this lining.
Measuring and materials
Measure the
joist bay

width at three points; it will not be perfectly consistent. Joist centres are typically 400 mm or 600 mm. The insulation must fit snugly without needing compression. For PIR board, cut to 5 mm less than the bay width; the friction-fit and gravity hold it in place. For mineral wool batts, cut to the exact bay width: the batts should be slightly oversize so they press against the joists without sagging.
Step by step
Check the existing ceiling lining for fire resistance. Tap the ceiling: 12.5 mm plasterboard sounds solid. Expose a corner to confirm thickness and material if in doubt.
If the ceiling is inadequate, fix 12.5 mm plasterboard directly to the joist soffits. Tape and joint all seams. Do not leave gaps at the perimeter where the ceiling meets the walls.

Cut PIR boards or mineral wool batts to fit each joist bay. For PIR, cut with a fine-toothed saw or a Stanley knife and straightedge. For mineral wool, use a long bread knife or insulation knife. Wear gloves and a dust mask with mineral wool.

Push PIR boards up into each bay, foil face up. They friction-fit against the joist sides. Use offcuts of timber nailed to the joist bottom edge as retaining clips if the fit is not snug enough to hold without slipping.
Fit a
vapour control layer

over the insulation on the warm side if using mineral wool: roll 250-micron polyethylene sheet from the house wall across the joist tops to the garage wall, lapping 150 mm up the walls. This is above the joists, so you need brief access from the living room floor above. For PIR board, the foil facing acts as the vapour check and no additional membrane is required.
Seal the perimeter of the insulation where it meets the walls using expanding foam. The gap between the joist end and the block wall is typically 20 to 40 mm; fill it completely.
What it looks like done well

Done well: every joist bay is filled to depth, the perimeter is sealed with foam, and the fire-resistant lining is continuous. The floor above feels warm to the foot. The garage ceiling sits near outdoor temperature in winter without chilling the room above.
Done badly: batts sag out of half the bays within a year. Perimeter gaps stay open. The ceiling lining has no fire rating. The fire risk is higher and the thermal gain is small.

attrs.symptom: PIR boards keep dropping out of joist bays

content.text: The boards are cut too narrow. Add a 10 mm foam strip to each joist edge to reduce the bay width before pushing in the board. Alternatively, staple a strip of retaining mesh to the joist bottoms.

attrs.symptom: Condensation dripping from joist underside after fitting

content.text: The vapour control layer is missing or incomplete. Warm moist air from the living room is diffusing through the floor and condensing on the cold underside of the joists. Re-open the floor from above and fit a continuous polyethylene membrane.
